CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The libeller of Canon M‘Inerney devoted its leading
article of Friday last to the Irish Language Movement.
Certainly we are getting ahead. The gloater over the
proposed robbing of Treland of 22 Parliamentary seats
says of the Language Movement that it has been
raised “ to the position of a national problem.””  Oh,

" Poor Sir John Arnott, what is this wicked world com-
ing to? .
5% The following is almost pathetic:— As a nation we
" 00 often allow ourselyes to be bullied by small minori-
ties with strong convictions. Just now many of us are
~ allowing ourselves to be bullied into accepting the Irish
language.””  Poor old Foz, the Irish dogs are heart-
lessly worrying it.
~ Some people are cunning, but some others are just a
little bit oo cunning. ~ Whether does the following
stamp the libeller of Canon M‘Inerney as cunning or
cunning :— We noed not say that we have no feel-
y to the Irish language.” Indeed, you
ot say it, Alf, for if you said it a thqusun_d times

The day after last St. Patrick’s Day the libeller of
Canon MInerney also devoted its leading article to the
Trish Language Question. It then “went bald-headed’”
for it,  IHore is an extract by way of refreshing the
careless memory of our contemporary :—* ‘We are now
witnessing in Ireland a strenuously supported crusade
for the revival of a tongue which, if not absolutely
dead, was most certainly sleeping. Such a phenomenon as
this is certainly worthy of careful study. It must of
necessity, if attended by any permanent measure of suc-
cess, raise a further barrier between the two divided
camps in this country, for it is inconceivable that Irish
will ever become the language of the North.”” It will
not be a bad time for Ireland when circumstances are
such that the libeller of Canon M‘Inerney will fall back
in good order and without the loss of a pound of type
from Westmoreland Kop to make a last stand for anti-
Irish Ascendancy at Sandy Row.

The New Ross Board of Guardians are at variance
with the deservedly unpopular Local Government
Board, or rather, we should say, that that Board is ah
variance with the New Ross Guardians. Mr. John
Cummins, in his breezy way, said:—“He thought the
time was come for the clected Boards throughout the
country to declare against the impertinent high-
handedness of the big-salaried gawks who were put to
rule over them in Dublin.”” Later on Mr. Cummins
said :— Here were the clected representatives of the
people doing the business of the country from year’s
end to year’s end, attending meetings at enormous ex-
pense and trouble, and here were their orders ignored
again and again. In fact, the local Boards could
order what they like, but the Local Government Board
asserted the power of preventing them doing anything
they did not like. The local Board might order the
most reasonable thing, but the Local Government
Board stepped in and prevented the order being car-
ried out. But any fad any of the Local Government
Board’s inspectors pointed out in the way of work on
the house—there was no question at all about sanction-
ing that. The Local Government Board, he supposed,
would carry on in this despotic way just as long as the
elected Boards of the country would put up with them.
1t was time for some Board to take the initiative, and
he would propose a resolution on the subject..”’

We are not minutely informed as to the merits of the
particular matters in dispute; what we are interested
in is the spirit of revolt, made articulate by Mr. John
Cummins, displayed in an elected body against the
meddling of a nominated and irresponsible Dublin
Castle body like the Local Government Board. It is
not a working arrangement that the people having been
granted a modicum of local government should be
dominecred by a  non-ropresentative, nominated
Dublin Castle, Board. The situation contains the
gorms  of rovolt, and revolt is inevitable.  When
olected minor bodies were created the days of a nomi-
nated “suporior”’ body were numbered. The harder
and the oftener the clected bodies strike at the Dublin
Castle Board the better.

Toachers and students of Irish will be glad to learn
that a text-book (* Cainnt ') will soon be issued which
must greatly assist both parties. . “Cainnt’’ is a
geries of conversational lessons specially arranged, ac-
cording to a unique and practical method, to suit the
particular requirements of oral instruction in class.
The importance of teaching Irish orally is now becom-
ing more and more recognised, but teachers have found
that the first thing necessary in this respect is a work-
able text-book framed on oral lines. With ¢ Cainnt

The Rev. Father O'Leary praises it very

"The hints about teaching are splendid." 
" Cainnt " will, we foa.:rn, be a.vailable n.bout the end of

There are two interesting items of information in the 
jjfayo 1Ycws of September 2nd. 
One is that some un-
fortunate man was charged at a special court in Ballina 
with having stolen a five-pound note from a solicitor. 
He is now in jail awaiting his trial at Quarter Sessions 
for the alleged offence. 
In the column to the imme-
diate left, there is an account of the " wire me £5 '' 
swindle about which newspaper readers are already ac-
quainted. 
Some swindler in London, it will be remem-
bered, wired to a gentleman in Westport--" Wire me 
five pounds--." The signature purported to be that 
of a friend of the Westport man. The money was for-
warded. 
On the following day the trick was tried on 
two other Westport men, bi.tt it failed. 
The man in 
London whose name was forged to the first and success-
ful request for £5 placed the matter in the llands of 
the police. It appears that the mean swindler is well 
known. 
The Mayo News says:-" It is also said that 
the £5 has been restored by the scamp's friends, and 
that probably the incident will not find its way into the 
criminal courts." 
The poor devil who is alleged to 
have stolen the £5 from the solicitor is in jail awaiting 
his trial; the swindling scamp, it appears, is to escape 
the criminal courts; his friends have refunded the five 
pounds. 
Is it not strange 1 
Hardly! 
The fact is, 
that the swindler is a " gentleman " well known to the 
people of Westport; the police know where he is. 
The 
swindler is "saved," and several of his friends are, it 
is said, in positions of influence in the secret society by 
the name of the Free and Accepted Masons of Ireland; 
the other unfortunate man who is in jail awaiting his 
trial for the alleged robbery of £5 is an " Idolator."

Many people and institutions have reason. to regret,
that the prize results of the Intermediate are once again
given to the p\lbl_u‘i 5 many, on the other hand, have

In the course of a leading article the Irish T'imes
says:“In the Experimental Science Course (Middle
Grade) the first exhibition is awarded to Mr. Morgan
F. Costelloe, Christian Schools, Limerick. The whole
of the ten exhibitions have gone to Roman Catholic

“ schools, which is as creditable to them as it is remark-

able. In the Classical Course of the Junior Grade the
first exhibition was won by Master Hyman Edelstein,
The High School, Dublin, and the second by Master
James E. Davey, Campbell College, Belfast.  There
are twenty-five exhibitions in this course, of which
eighteen have been taken by Roman Catholic and seven
by Protestant schools. In the Modern Literary Course
the first exhibition goes to Master Michael J. Hogan,
Christian Schools, North Richmond Street, Dublin,
and the second to Master Patrick Dargan, of the same
schools. Thirty-seven exhibitions were given, of which
no less than thirty-six have gone to Roman Catholic
schools. This result is a very surprising one, and will,
no doubt, give rise to considerable searchings of heart
in the Protestant schools.”’

The Irish Times has to face an ugly state of affaits,
if it is not ungallant to put it that way, when
it turns to the girls. The libeller of Canon
M‘Inerney says:—In the Modern Liferary Course the
first exhibition was won by Miss Elizabeth Devine,
Dominican College; Eccles Street, Dublin, and the
second by Miss Hannah Hurley, from the same college.
Of the eleven awarded ten were won by the pupils of
Roman Catholic institutions.”” And again:—“ As
happened in the Seniox Grade no exhibitions were con-

ferred in the Classical or in the first rank of the Modern
Literary Course of the Middle Grade. In the second
class the first exhibition was won by Miss Eveleen Hug-
gard, Alexandra College, Dublin, and the second by
Miss Honor Sullivan, Loreto College, Stephen’s Green.
Of the thirteen exhibitions given in this course ten
were credited to Roman Catholic and three to Protes-
tant schools.””

In the course of its analysis and comments thereon
the Zrish 7'mes says:—"* Turning to the girls’ lists one
is at once struck with the unexampled successes of the
Convent Schools. How such an improvement has been
offected during the four years which have elapsed since
last publication of the lists should be a subject of
anxious thought, especially for the Ulster ladies’
schools, to whose pupils so many of these prizes were
annually awarded. It is true that Vietoria College,
Belfast, and Victoria High School, Londonderry, still
occupy prominent places in these records; but the fact
that they have both been surpassed, both in total of
cxhibitions and in total of awards by the Dominican
Convent, Eccles Street, and St. Louis’ Convent,
Monaghan, calls on them to be up and stirring, and if
they have rclaxed any of their efforts in the past to
strive strenuously to regain their position in the near
future. That only one exhibition in the Senior Grade,
out of thirteen awarded, should have been gained by a
Protestant student, and that by a pupil of Rochelle
Seminary, Cork, is a fact which needs no comment of
ours to point a moral; and in the Middle Grade things
are little better.””  That is no pleasant reading for
England’s Faithful Garrison in this country.

In the course of a letter the Hon. Secretaries of the
forthcoming Ulster Gaelic League Conference write:—
“TIt has long been apparent to workers in the language
movement in the North that, while excellent work is
being done in particular districts, and while isolated
branches and workers are carrying on the fight success-
fully, still, if contrasted with the other provinces,
Ulster is lamentably deficient in organisation. Mun-
ster and Connaught have their training colleges, while
Leinster has a school of Irish learning, and in each
there is sufficient cohesion among the constituent parts
of the League to enable combined action to be taken on
any question of moment. In Ulster the scattered units
have never been brought in touch with one another.
In addition, the necessity for a training college is evi-
denced by the scarcity of competent teachers to give in-
struction in the native speech either in the schools or in
the capacity of travelling teachers.”” A Conference is
to be held towards the end of this month at Derry, and
the following matters will be considered :—(1) The es-
tablishment of a training college in Ulster; (2) the
creation of machinery for the more efficient organisa-
tion of Ulster; (3) the cultivation of the language and
literature in Ulster. The Hon. Secretaries, with whom
those interested should put themselves in communica-
tion, are Miss Lavery, 18 King St., Belfast; Fa-
ther M. O'Mullin, C.C., Carndonagh, Co. Donegal,
and Mr. P. J. Flanagan, Derry People, Derry.

We are always interested to hear of an Irishman re-
turning to Ireland determined to work out his living
here. Mr. McKee of Messrs. McKee and Co., 4 South
William Street, Dublin, is a young Irishman who has
come back from America with this intention, and we
wish him success.  Under the above title he has
started a factory for paper bags and a business in the
printing, stationery, and bookbinding line.  Irish Ive-
Janders will be also interested to learn that he has laid
in a stock of Trish type and Irish stationery.

Amongst the forthcoming books announced for the
autumn 1s a novel by Miss Louise Kenny, “The Red-
Haired Woman: Her Autobiography.”” The book will
be published by John Murray. Miss Kenny has made
a considerable reputation by her contributions to vari-
ous magazines and reviews, and under a pen-name she
hag contributed from time to time several very interest-
ing articles to the LEADER.

The records of Irish journalism are strewn with
failures in the way of illustrated comic journals. Un-
daunted by the fate of other ventures 7'he Leprechaun,
a monthly illustrated comic, made a start four months
ago. And it is still very much alive.  7'he Leprechaun
has no gospel to preach; its mission is to malke its
readers laugh, and it is emphatically evident that our
friend, Mr. Bung, is welcome at so much an inch in
the advertising enclosures. The drawings of “Fitz,”
needless to say, are invariably clever.  The Freeman
versus the Independent Cartoon in the August Number
made us laugh, and it ought to have come as a welcome
comic relief even to the over-worked challenging-mana-
ger of the latter organ. There is one great fact in
fayour of Zhe Leprechaun: Ireland is becoming some-

CORRESPONDENCE.

DUNGARVAN AND BETTING.

Sir,—I read with much interest the generally ac-
curate, if somewhat impressionist, sketches of the De-
cies which “ Mapban’ is at present contributing to thp
Leaper; and I trust it will not be considered as evi-
dence of unwillingness to sitbmit to honest, though
severe, criticism if I express my dissent from one very
sweeping and—unintentionally, T presumec—offensive
statement which he makes in your issuc of this week.

Speaking of Dungarvan, “Mapba:’’ says:—“For
the rest, some 50 per cent. of male population between
20 and 30 live by betting.”” As a native cf the town,
I regret very much that so grave a reflection on its
young men should be given currency to in your journal,
and, believing it to be entirely false, I cannot permit
it to go uncontradicted. ~Though no longer resident
there I still keep in touch with the place, and I may be
presumed to know as much of the social life of the
people as could be learned by your contributor during a
passing visit.

In my opinion, his assertion, in reference to the bet-
ting habits of a portion of the male population, is a
gross exaggeration—so gross as to be absurd.

To me the betting evil secems to be no more developed
in Dungarvan than it is in other small towns of the
South; and I shall be very much surprised, indeed, to
learn that even five persons make a livelihood by so pre-
carious and so undesirable a method.

‘What T state is merely an opinion—and the opinion of
one who only occasionally visits his native place; but I
trust some responsible person, living in the town and
possessed of a more intimate knowledge of its ways than
either Mapvan or I can lay claim to, will come for-
ward and give us‘an authoritative statement on this

subject.—I am, sincerely yours, “LEo.”

\THE INDUSTRIAL CONFERENCE.

NE of the matters to be discussed at the forthcom-
ing Industrial Conference at Cork is, “ The
question of all Irish public Boards giving a decided pre-
ference for the next five years to goods of Irish manu-
facture, cven though, at the moment, some of them
may be slightly higher in price, but not inferior in
quality to similar imported goods This with the ob-
ject of enabling deserving struggling industries to ad-
vance, and give more employment to Irish hands.”
Now we hope we will not be considered as intruding
prematurely if we venture to make some remarks on
this matter. Our readers know that not even one of
the many manufacturers who replied to a letter we sent
out some months ago claimed anything in the way of
a financial grant-in-aid from his customers. We
thought that for all practical purposes the unanimous
verdict of the large number of these manufacturers,
many of them in the very first rank of Irish industry,
would have decided that controversy. Iowever, it is
to be raised again in a somewhat restricted form at the
forthcoming Conference at Cork.

Let us first have a glance at the subject matter of the
proposed discussion.  The proposed grant-in-aid is to
be confined to public Boards that spend the people’s
and not their own money. The time is to be limited to

five years. The degree of grant-in-aid is not stated;
it is left in vagueness by the phrase “slightly
higher;”” and it will be observed that though

a “slightly higher’’ price may be entertained there is
to be no tampering in the matter of “ quality.”’ Why
not same price though “slightly lower '" quality? This
vague, though well-intentioned, sentiment, generated
in an open boiler, it is, no doubt, hoped may help “ de-
serving ’’ and “struggling ’’ industries. Who is to de-
cide  what particular industries are “deserving’’ and
likewise “ struggling ¥"*

On the matter of “as good and as cheap,”” we are not
cast iron doctrinaires; we look at the question as men
of calculation who desire to get the most pressure in
favour of Irish industry out of the natural and na-
tional sentiment for Irish industry. Our position is
that an industrial movement based, in so far as the
consumer is concerned, on the principles that an Irish-
man is foolish, an enemy to his own interests as well as
unpatriotic; who does not give a preference to Irish
goods, and go to some trouble that involves no pecu-
niary sacrifice in order to do so—such a movement is on
a solid business foundation ; that from that foundation
it is possible to enlist the hard-headed men in their
capacity as consumers to rally to the support of Irish
industry ; and incidentally we may remark that a move-
ment based on that foundation will probably carry with
it all those, be they few or many, who are anxious to
give grants-in-aid to Irish products. On the other
hand, a movement based on the consumer giving grants-
in-aid would be scoffed out of existence, would present
Irish industry as a mendicant, would increase the
existing prejudice against Irish products, would
frighten off capital still more—and we all know that

almost entirely removed that impression.

has no more claim t·o its peculiar title than Bundoran

« All the same, I think a fixed scale (even for trips out of
the beaten track) would commend itself, and the boat-
men would gain in the long run. However, oven an
army of would-be extortionate boatmen—and I only
meb a couple—would not justify Killarney’s reproach-
ful title “ of the Beggars.” Highway robbery may be
disagreeable, but it is not mendicancy.

But were there no beggars at all?~ Oh, yes; a fow.
A friend of _ine suggests that a naturally forbidding
exterior gained me a blessed immunity. I am too
mo(.iesb to do more than mildly doubt his suggoestion.
fI.‘wn.ce, at least, near the Cathedral T was urged to “lave
4 pinny wud a poor 'oman, an’ God Almighty bless
you!”  The startling thing about these requests was
that they came from very comfortable-looking old ones.
In the dirty cobble paved lanes that led off the Iigh St.
I saw many a wretched poor creature whose request
would have moved me, but no request was made. Some-
times at dusk in a tree-bordered road near the Cathe-
dral, a few small boys, one of whom was armed with a
nerve-distracting mouth-organ, would offer to dance an
Tvish j for a consideration, of course. But then a
circus was coming to the town, and they were despe-
rately hard set to raise the price of a seat. In the Gap
there were a few old ones with wisps of white heather,
and baskets containing leathery-skinned oranges and
bottles of soda-water. Perhaps they would resent being
called beggars, but their scraggy heather and tough
fruit were alinost as much a blind as the bootlaces or
matches of the town mendicant. However, they were
not too pressing, and a few pence contented them.
Perhaps the most unpleasant beggar I met in all my
time in Killarney was the very pretty girl who came
from a cottage in the Gap, and demanded silver for my
“footing.”” The demand was accompanied with gross
and lavish compliments. It hurt one’s pride sorely to
think of these same compliments being plastered by a
fine-looking Trish girl over every vulgar tripper of the
other sex who came that way.

On the day of the Sports that preceded the Munster
Feis I walked out to the grounds, beside the Flesk, and
found only one “dark’’ woman begging by the road-
side. I must say I was a little surprised. The sight-
seers assembled were not numerous, but all the same I
had expected to see more of “the broken ones of the
earth *’ lining the way and clamouring for alms. Once
on a race day at Mullingar I counted twenty beggars
along the canal bank hetween the town and the course.
They were sitting or standing by the hedge, in many
cases exhibiting their deformities nakedly, and chorus-
sing strident appeals for pity mingled with prayers that
were often grotesque—a painful and repulsive spectacle.
And at Killarney— Killarney of the Beggars *’—there
was but one poor blind creature with outstretched hand
and pathetic face to bescech an alms.

Beside that same Flesk, however, and on that same
Sunday morning, I had a surprising experience of beg-
ging. "I was leaning over the rugged parapet watching
the brown water singing over the pebbles when a decent,
well-dressed old man came along.  The following dia-
logue ensued :—

He: “Fine day!”

1: “Fine day, then.”

He: “Grand weadher!”

I: “Aye, indeed, thanks be to God.”

He (insinuatingly): “Iave ’ou any money for the
tobacky ¥’

T (surprised, and looking hard at the good clothes of
him): “No, then, not a ha’penny.” A 5

He (taken aback): ‘ Wisha, that the Lord mayn’t
lave you long so!”” ]

A silence of a few moments, during which I cou-

~tinued to take stock of his comfortable rig-out.

He (encouragingly): “If ‘ou a fow pince I'd be say-
in’ the prayers for ‘ou.” ;

Another silence, during which I regard him stonily.

He (with a last effort): “Two or three pince now—
do.”

T shake my head, and he moves off, muttering some

prayers I have not paid for.
These fow instances I have given are all that occurred

~ during ten days, during which I was out of doors most

of the time. I am not entering a defence of cadging
in itself; I merely want to say that, as I saw it, Killar-
ney is no more a place of beggars than any other tourist
resort in Ireland—or out of it, probably. There are
many other things I might say of Killarney, but here
I am only concerned with its beggars.

THE LIFE OF ST. PATRICK AND HIS PLACE
IN HISTORY.*

" HE announcement which was made some months
back that a work by Professor Bury on “ The
Life of St. Patrick—his place in history,”” was in pre-
paration for publication, excited much interest in
literary civcles. This was naturally so. The high posi-
tion of Mr. Bury as Regius Professor of Modern History
at Cambridge, and his reputation as the author of
“ History of the Later Roman Empire,”” justified great
expectations. It was hoped that an independent and
exhaustive examination of sources would be followed
by a careful sifting of the evidence separating the
wheat from the chaff, and formulating conclusions with
decision and precision. This, however, was not to be.
The professor has chosen to adopt a lighter and more
airy method, moving as it were on the wing over the
field of enquiry, picking up a fact here, passing over a
fact there, and soaring a little at times into the fascina-
ting region of questionable conjectures. I cannot of
course follow him in his flight page by page or chapter
by chapter, but must confine myself to giving a few
illustrative details. ~ St. Patrick does not state in so
many words where he was born, but says:—“T had for
my father Calpurnius, a deacon (Decurio?), who lived
at the Viens Bannavem of Tabernia. For he had a small
estate hard by where I was taken prisoner when I was
nearly sixteen years of age. I knew not God truly, and
I was brought captive to Ireland with many thousands
as we deserved for we had fallen away from God and
not kept his commandments and were not obedient to
our priests who admonished us for our salvation.”
This extract from the confession of St. Patrick proves
(1) that there was a Christian community of many
thousands (excluding the very young and the very old)
at the place where he was captured, under the spiritual
guidance of priests; and (2) that there was a City
Council or Senate there, the members of which were
decurions. This place of capture is now regarded uni-
versally as the place of birth. It was supposed by
many writers during the past fifty years that Dumbarton

modern archwological research in Great Britain would
now assert that there ever was a City Council and
decurions North of the Tweed (test No. I.), or that
there was at Dumbarton during the infancy of St.
Patrick a Christian population of many thousands (test
No. II.). Applying test No. IT. Professor Bury says
that Alclinth, “the Rock of Clyde,” at the extreme
end of the Northern wall, is the last place we would
oxpect to find the villula of a Roman Decurio; and the
opinion that the home of Calpurnius was in that remote
spot cannot be accepted without better evidence than an
anonymous statement (z.e., of the Scholiast) which we
cannot trace to any trustworthy source’’—(page 324).
The Professor’s own view has manifestly been obtained
from one of his excursions into the region of probabili-

H A VE read with varied feelings the criticism by 
Mr . Louis vVa.Jsh of a story by Seumas Mac-
Man us, and the response of the author. 
When people 
commence to argue on a subject I have noticed that 
they can rea.Jly convince themselves of anything; I am 
not at all surprised to find Seumas MacManus in the 
heat of argument, declaring that he wrote of Billy 
Ba.:s:t er in a, spirit of love. 
But though I am convinced 
that he is convinced he did so, I am not convinced of 
the fact. 
I hope everyone can follow this argument. 
I know Mr. MacManus to be incapable of asserting an 
untruth, and at the same time, with my faith in his 
veracity unshaken, I don't believe a word of it. He is 
like t he demonstrator of a Euclid problem, who for sake 
of argument assumes that A and B are two equal straight 
lines, when it is plain to all beholders that they are 
nothing of the sort ; and yet for the sake of argument 
it is granted. 
In the same way I grant that I believe Seumas Mac-
Manus incapable of writing in a spirit of mockery about 
the manners of t he Irish peasantry, and at the same 
time the fact remains that in Billy Baxter, he has 
drawn a very vulgar and ridiculous specimen. 
H is 
strai<rht line is crooked, t hough he did not mean it 
to bi, and the cause of the crookedness possibly is, 
that he has been sketching from nature, from human 
nature which even in Ireland abounds in crookedness 
and a good deal of vulgarity. 
Now I must shift the argument from the scene of 
that New York dinner table, where I never met Billy 
Baxter- in other words I have not read that story ; 
but Billy figures in another story by the same author 
en'titled " The Subscription Horse." 
In this Billy is . 
the impersonation of a rough, outspoke1;, unfeeling 
specimen of a North of IreJand man at his vulgarest. 
And yet I must say that I can read this tale with more 
pleasu~·e (though . of a different. sort) than I ,would ex-
. patience m readm_g ?ne of F10na Macf:eod s or Mr. 
Yeat's tales descriptive of the moonstncken peasant 
of the Celtic renaissance type. 
A penny story full of 
human nature, written with no other object than to 
make the reader laugh, is nothing to be despised nor 
ashamed of.

At the same time there is no doubt that he is 
occasionally led away into mere extravaganza, and I 
don't see that he has any right to be unclul_y shock~d 
at the accusation. 
He is surely not unconscious of it. 
He is most at his ease, perhap~, when th.is ex~rav~-
ganza is permissible and can be given free rem as m _his 
narration of folk tales, like " Jack and the Lord High 
Mayor of Dublin," or the "Wisdom of Dark Patrick." 
but occasionally ho has written pages of blended hu-
mour and pathos, in which his use of the North of 
Ireland vernacular makes him a master of realism. 
A 
brother of Billy Baxter's, a real North of I reland type, 
appears in the person of " Tumrnus,'' the gardener in 
Miss Alexander's delightful book, 
'' Lady Anne's 
Vvalk." " Tummus" is a typical Northern in speech 
and manner, never unbending in any abject civility., 
even to his young lady, though she is the mistress of 
the A1·ch-E,piscopal Palace of Protestant Ireland. • 
Bluntly he prefaces all his remarks to her: - " Do ye-
know what I'm gain' to t ell ye." 
His wife is curtly 
alluded to as " Thon widely woman," because she is 
number two, although she has been for over twen'u)' 
years his helpmeet . Then read his description of the 
" Battle of Scarva." It is si,s extravagantly funny and 
even rough as anything Seumas MacManus ever wrote, 
and yet it is from the pen of a refined writer and cul-
tured lady; but it is a piece of realism. 
For indeed it 
does not follow that because Ireland is going to be 
Irish, she will cease altogether from being comical. 
Has not the stage Irishman, with all his drolleries and 
some of his absurdities, triumphed for the past few 
years in the little comedies of the Gaelic Revival. 
,v hat about the scene in " An Doctuir," where there 
is so much ado about the wearing of a collar ; is that 
any worse than Billy Baxter in his shirt sleeves in New 
York? 
If the " ,vake of the Barrel," or " I'm in Arrear3," 
were sung iu E nglish, would they not be denounced in

. 
I am not for banishing broad comedy from the pro-
paganda platform of the Gaelic Movement, but let us 
have everyhing in proportion. 
Oomecly is a part of 
life.

. Bub of course to have realism, the comedy should be
in proportion, and the spirit of racial pride, or as-
sumed racial superiority, should not be allowed to up-
set the balance. In conclusion, I am of opinion that
Billy Baxter with all his vulgarity, is a true if dis-
agreeable type of an Ivishman. It is allowable for
Mr. MacManus to exhibit him and other such types,
but with discretion, not too frequently lest it should
come to be thought that the type was as frequent in
Irish life as in the story books.

Then you, Mr. LeapERr, should look facts in the face.
‘Why object to pictures of Irishmen driving pigs, or
to bog-oak carvings of those animals? You have at-
tacked many real abuses, notably the drink evil; then
why waste your time and wits in trying to persuade
people that a picture of Paddy driving a pig is a
libel on our nation? If you leave Dublin and re-visit
th_e haunts of your youth, you will discover that in
spite of your crusade against all such absurdities, that
useful animal and his dviver (very frequently answer-
ing to the name of Patrick) are.no more out of date
than that political party over whose extinction you re-
cently rejoiced.

AvicE L. MILLIGAN.

[We are not parties in this controversy; but inci-
dentally, and in the last paragraph, Miss Milligan
drags us into it for a moment by referring to our treat-
ment of Paddy and his pig post-card and bog oak atro-
cities. Let us say that what we particularly object to
in this traffic is neither the pig—an animal that stands
for a large proportion of our national wealth—nor
Paddy; we particularly object to Paddy engaged in
idiotic converse with a pig as to the number of miles
to Cork and that sort of thing, which in the form of
post-cards and bog oak manipulations, are placed on
the Irish market for the benefit of the British and
other Tourists.—ED. LEADER.]
